Pelvic anatomy

True pelvis and false pelvis

False pelvis

Part of the pelvis above the linea terminalis is false pelvis

Boundaries

  • Posteriorly - lumbar vertebra
  • Laterally - iliac fossa
  • Anteriorly - lower abdominal wall

True pelvis

Boundaries

  • Anteriorly - upper margin of pubis
  • Laterally - linea terminalis
  • Posteriorly - sacral promontory and alae of sacrum

Linea terminalis

Innominate line Consists of

  • Pectineal line (pecten pubis)
  • Arcuate line
  • Pubic crest
  • Sacral ala, and
  • Sacral promontory

It forms the boundary of the pelvic inlet

Cavity of true pelvis

Cavity of true pelvis is bent anteriorly

Posterior wall is ~ 10 cm and anterior wall ~5 cm

Walls of the true pelvis

Posterior boundary - sacrum

Lateral walls - inner surface of the ischial bones and the Sacro sciatic notches and ligaments

Anterior boundary - pubic bones, the ascending superior rami of the ischial bones, and the obturator foramen

Linea terminalis - pelvic brim, edge of pelvic inlet

Diameters

Shortest diameter of the pelvic cavity

Shortest diameter of the pelvic cavity - distance between the ischial spines

Ischial spines - landmark for assessing the level to which the presenting part of the foetus during labour

Pubic arch

Inferior rami of the pubic bones - forms 90 to 110 round arch

Pelvic joints

Symphysis pubis

  • Joint in the midline between two pubic bones
  • Consists of fibrocartilage and superior + inferior pubic ligaments
  • Arcuate ligament of the pubis = inferior pubic ligament

Sacroiliac Joints

  • Joint between the sacrum and the iliac portion of the pelvic bones
  • Movements - Nutation and ante-nutation

Joints are relaxed by relaxin

Pelvic planes and diameters

  • Pelvic Inlet
  • Mid pelvis
  • Pelvic Outlet

Pelvic Inlet

Boundaries

  • Posterior - promontory and alae of the sacrum
  • Lateral - linea terminalis
  • Anterior - horizontal pubic rami and the symphysis pubis

Diameters of the pelvic inlet

Anteroposterior diameter

  • Shortest distance between the promontory of the sacrum and the symphysis pubis
  • Also referred as obstetrical conjugate
  • Obstetrically most important

Transverse diameter

  • Greatest distance between the linea terminalis on either side
  • Constructed at right angles to the anteroposterior diameter

Two oblique diameters

  • From one of the sacroiliac synchondroses to the iliopectineal eminence on the opposite side

Obstetrical conjugate

Shortest anteroposterior diameter through which the head must pass in descending through the pelvic inlet. Obstetrical conjugate cannot be measured directly

Diagonal conjugate

Distance from the lower margin of the symphysis to the promontory of the sacrum

Measurement

Diagonal conjugate is measured through per-vaginal examination.

Coccyx mobility is examined and later sacrum is palpated from below upwards.

Usually, only the last three sacral vertebrae can be felt without indenting the perineum

Obstetrical conjugate is computed by subtracting 1.5 to 2.0 cm

True conjugate

The anteroposterior diameter of the pelvic inlet

It is not the shortest diameter of the pelvic inlet

Midpelvis

Plane of least pelvic dimensions

Measured at the level of the ischial spines

Interspinous diameter is usually the smallest diameter of the pelvis

Pelvic Outlet

Has two triangles facing opposite directions with a common base

Posterior apex - tip of sacrum

Lateral - sacrosciatic ligaments and the ischial tuberosities

Anterior - pubic arch

Pelvic shapes

  • Gynecoid
  • Anthropoid
  • Anthropoid
  • Platypelloid

Engagement

Descent of the biparietal plane of the fetal head to a level below that of the pelvic inlet

Once, the fetal head engages, it proves that the pelvic inlet is ample for that foetus

Clinical importance

  • Women with the wider pelvis and a shorter obstetric conjugate are found to be associated with increased risk of pelvic floor disorders later in life.
  • These women often have difficult labour that predisposes them to development of pelvic floor neuropathy or pelvic floor prolapse.

Surgically important ligaments

Sacrospinous Ligament

  • Extends from the ischial spine to sacrum
  • Pudendal nerve and internal pudendal nerves pass deep to this ligament
  • Clinical importance: sacrospinous ligament is used for the vaginal suspension

Sacrotuberous Ligament

  • Extends from ischial tuberosity to lateral aspect of the sacrum
  • Merges medially with the sacrospinous ligament
  • Pudendal nerve and internal pudendal vessels lie anterior to the sacrospinous ligament

Foramina

Greater sciatic foramen

  • Structures passing through the greater sciatic foramen are
  • Piriformis
  • Superior gluteal nerves and vessels
  • Sciatic nerve
  • Nerve to obturator internus
  • Internal pudendal nerve and vessels

Lesser sciatic foramen

  • Structures passing through this foramen are
  • Tendon of obturator internus
  • Nerve to obturator internus
  • Pudendal vessels and nerve reenter pelvis

Muscles of pelvis

Lateral pelvic wall muscles

  • Piriformis
  • Obturator internus
  • Iliopsoas

Pelvic floor muscles

  • Pelvic diaphragm
  • levator ani and the coccygeus muscles
  • superior and inferior fasciae

Test Your Knowledge

Click on your answer to see if you're correct!

1. The false pelvis is located:
  • Below the linea terminalis
  • Above the linea terminalis
  • Within the pelvic outlet
  • At the level of ischial spines
2. The linea terminalis forms:
  • Pelvic outlet
  • Pelvic floor
  • Boundary of pelvic inlet
  • Boundary of pelvic outlet
3. The obstetrical conjugate is:
  • Longest diameter of pelvic inlet
  • Distance between ischial tuberosities
  • Shortest anteroposterior diameter of pelvic inlet
  • Distance between iliac crests
4. The narrowest diameter of the pelvis is:
  • Transverse diameter of inlet
  • Oblique diameter
  • Interspinous diameter
  • True conjugate
5. Engagement of fetal head refers to:
  • Passage through pelvic outlet
  • Descent below pelvic inlet of biparietal diameter
  • Rotation in pelvic cavity
  • Expulsion of fetus
6. The sacrospinous ligament extends between:
  • Ischial tuberosity and sacrum
  • Ischial spine and sacrum
  • Pubis and sacrum
  • Iliac crest and sacrum
